title: Preventing flystrike in rabbits
subtitle: Daily hygiene checks and hutch management that stop blowfly strike before it starts.
excerpt: Flystrike can kill a rabbit within 24 hours of the first eggs being laid. The blowfly deposits eggs in damp, soiled, or wounded tissue around the hindquarters; the maggots hatch within twelve hours in warm weather and begin feeding on living tissue. Prevention is daily inspection, clean bedding, and prompt treatment of any diarrhoea or sore.
sourceNotes:
RSPCA, Welfare standards for rabbits (2019). https://www.rspca.org.uk
Veterinary Medicines Directorate, VetCompass fly-strike data. https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/veterinary-medicines-directorate

content.text: Between May and October, check the hindquarters of every rabbit at least once a day. Part the fur around the vent, tail, and inner thighs and look for any eggs (small white clusters, like fine breadcrumbs) or early maggots.
found in the first twelve hours is treatable; found after 24 hours, the prognosis is poor.

Any damp, soiled, or broken skin is a target for blowfly. The most common risk factors are: wet or fouled bedding from a leaking drinker or heavy droppings; soft droppings or diarrhoea (including uneaten
stuck to the coat); wounds, sore hocks, or skin folds in overweight rabbits; and a heavy-coated breed that traps moisture around the vent. Address each risk as soon as you identify it.

Remove soiled bedding from the cage floor daily in summer. A build-up of damp organic matter raises the ammonia level in the cage and attracts flies. Site cages away from the prevailing wind direction where flies congregate around manure storage. Fly screens on the shed windows reduce fly pressure without restricting ventilation.

A vet-prescribed spot-on (cyromazine), used every ten weeks from April to October, kills fly eggs and young larvae before they can break the skin. It does not replace daily checks. It gives a safety window on days when you cannot look the rabbit over as closely as you would like.

attrs.body: If you find maggots already in the skin, do not attempt home treatment. Clip the fur from the affected area, keep the rabbit warm, and call the vet immediately. Maggots penetrate deeply and produce toxins that cause shock. The vet will remove larvae, debride the wound, and prescribe antibiotics and fluid support. Treatment within two to four hours of discovery gives the best prognosis.
attrs.title: When to call the vet
